essential_question: Why can't a fraction be left with ‚àö2 in the denominator ‚Äî and what fixes it without changing its value?
objective: Rationalize a denominator containing a single radical by multiplying numerator and denominator by that radical, simplify the leftover radicand when needed, and combine two rationalized fractions using a common denominator.

content.prompt: A calculator says 1/‚àö2 ‚âà 0.7071. Multiply 1/‚àö2 by ‚àö2/‚àö2 ‚Äî which equals 1 ‚Äî by hand or on a calculator. What happens to the denominator? Does the decimal value change at all?

content.heading: The radical always leaves the bottom
bullets: Rationalizing a denominator means rewriting a fraction so NO radical sign is left on the bottom ‚Äî multiply top and bottom by the SAME radical that's in the denominator.
Since ‚àön/‚àön = 1, you're only ever multiplying by 1 ‚Äî the value never changes: 1/‚àö2 = (1√ó‚àö2)/(‚àö2√ó‚àö2) = ‚àö2/2.
The radical always cancels because ‚àön √ó ‚àön = n, a whole number every time: 9/‚àö2 = 9‚àö2/2; 10/‚àö5 = 10‚àö5/5 = 2‚àö5 (check whether it reduces!).
Sometimes the leftover radicand STILL has a perfect-square factor hiding inside ‚Äî simplify that too: 3/‚àö8 = 3‚àö8/8 = 3(2‚àö2)/8 = 6‚àö2/8 = 3‚àö2/4.
To add or subtract two radical fractions, rationalize EACH one first, THEN find a common denominator ‚Äî never the other way around: 3/‚àö2 + 5/‚àö8 = 3‚àö2/2 + 5‚àö2/4 = 11‚àö2/4.

content.heading: Step through fractions and watch the denominator clear
content.note: On the sim, step through the preset fractions (or type your own numerator and radicand) and watch the multiply-by-‚àön/‚àön step build to the rationalized, simplified result. Then use Check Yourself to type your own rationalized fraction and see if it's fully reduced. Switch to Combine mode and rationalize both fractions before deciding whether they can combine.

content.preview: Build session: card-sort un-rationalized fractions to their clean forms, and measure why 1/‚àö2 and ‚àö2/2 land on the exact same mark.

success_criteria.text: I can rationalize a fraction with a single radical in the denominator by multiplying by that radical over itself
I can combine two radical fractions by rationalizing each one first, then finding a common denominator
I can recognize when the leftover radicand still needs simplifying, and finish simplifying it

questions.prompt: To rationalize the denominator of 7/‚àö5, you multiply the numerator and denominator by‚Ä¶
questions.explain: Multiplying top and bottom by ‚àö5 turns the denominator into ‚àö5 √ó ‚àö5 = 5, a whole number ‚Äî and since ‚àö5/‚àö5 = 1, the fraction's value never changes.

questions.prompt: Which fraction below still needs its denominator rationalized?
questions.explain: 5/‚àö6 still has a radical sitting in the denominator; the other two already show a whole number on the bottom.
Multiply top and bottom by ‚àö2: (8 √ó ‚àö2)/(‚àö2 √ó ‚àö2) = 8‚àö2/2, and 8 and 2 share a factor of 2, reducing to 4‚àö2.

questions.prompt: Rationalize and simplify: 5/‚àö12. (Careful ‚Äî the radicand isn't done simplifying after you rationalize.)
questions.explain: ‚àö12 simplifies to 2‚àö3 before the fraction reduces further; 10 and 12 share a factor of 2, giving the fully simplified 5‚àö3/6.

questions.prompt: Combine: 3/‚àö2 + 5/‚àö8. Rationalize each term first, then find the common denominator.
questions.explain: ‚àö8 simplifies to 2‚àö2, so 5/‚àö8 rationalizes to 5‚àö2/4. Both terms now share the radicand 2, so a common denominator of 4 lets them combine.

questions.prompt: A Lesson 20 leg length comes out to 10/‚àö2 before it's rationalized. Rationalize it to its exact, simplified form.
questions.explain: Multiply top and bottom by ‚àö2: 10‚àö2/2, and 10 √∑ 2 = 5, giving the clean exact leg length 5‚àö2 ‚Äî no fraction left at all.
